questions.text: I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that most clearly proves to me that moral obligations are not chosen but discovered.
I sense the unspoken loyalty lines in a workplace.
I prefer making decisions slowly and carefully over making them quickly.
I feel that I have a right to put myself first without apology.
I set specific, measurable objectives rather than vague aspirations.
I tend to assert my position firmly in discussions.
I find that emotional equilibrium during difficult times is a resource I can draw on consistently without it diminishing over time.
I find that prioritizing my own comfort over others' needs feels wrong.
I hold the view that true success is measured by how much you improve others' lives.
I find that I am able to maintain my composure naturally, without having to actively manage my reactions.
I am the kind of person who can be brought to tears by a commercial.
I pick up on the way someone's posture shifts when a particular person enters the room.
I often present my thoughts as starting points for group discussion.
I think it is important to make a stand and defend it.
I rarely notice when someone is building consensus behind the scenes before a formal discussion.
I focus fully on the current step and trust that the next step will become clear.
Even minor stressors can set off a cascade of intense internal sensations.
I believe that the moral demand of suffering is not something that can be satisfied by sympathy alone — it requires purposeful engagement.
